jsonData.question: Which of the following best defines cloud computing?
options: The on-demand delivery of IT resources over the internet with pay-as-you-go pricing
The practice of hosting all applications on dedicated physical servers owned by the organization
A model where applications run exclusively on private networks within a company's data center
The process of virtualizing physical servers to run multiple operating systems simultaneously
jsonData.explanation: Cloud computing is defined as the on-demand delivery of IT resources over the internet with pay-as-you-go pricing — you pay only for what you consume. The other options describe on-premises hosting, private cloud deployment, and server virtualization respectively, none of which capture the 'on-demand over the internet' aspect that defines cloud computing.

jsonData.question: A company must keep certain financial records on-premises due to government regulations, but wants to run its web application in the cloud. Which deployment model best fits this requirement?
options: Hybrid deployment, connecting cloud-based resources to on-premises infrastructure
Cloud-based deployment, where all application components run in the cloud
On-premises deployment using virtualization and resource management tools
Edge deployment using AWS Outposts installed at the company's data center
jsonData.explanation: Hybrid deployment connects cloud-based resources to on-premises infrastructure, making it ideal when regulations require certain records to remain on-premises while other workloads benefit from the cloud. A fully cloud-based deployment would violate the regulatory requirement. AWS Outposts is a mini-AWS Region on premises, but it's still owned/operated by AWS rather than solving a simple hybrid split.

jsonData.question: A company asks why AWS can offer lower per-unit prices than running their own data center at equivalent scale. Which benefit of cloud computing best explains this?
options: Economies of scale — AWS aggregates usage across hundreds of thousands of customers, lowering per-unit costs for everyone
The ability to deploy applications to multiple Regions worldwide in minutes
The elimination of upfront capital expenditures for hardware purchases
Auto Scaling prevents over-provisioning, reducing wasted spend on unused capacity
jsonData.explanation: Economies of scale is the specific benefit that explains lower pricing: because AWS serves hundreds of thousands of customers, it achieves purchasing power and operational efficiency no single company can match, passing savings on as lower pay-as-you-go prices. The other options describe 'go global in minutes,' 'trade upfront expense for variable expense,' and 'stop guessing capacity' — all valid benefits, but not the one explaining AWS's pricing advantage.

jsonData.question: What does Amazon EC2 provide?
options: A serverless function execution environment that runs code without server management
A fully managed container orchestration service based on Kubernetes
A dedicated physical server fully allocated to a single customer
Secure, resizable virtual servers (instances) in the cloud
jsonData.explanation: Amazon EC2 (Elastic Compute Cloud) provides secure, resizable virtual servers in the cloud. Option A describes AWS Lambda, option B describes Amazon EKS, and option C describes EC2 Dedicated Hosts — a specific EC2 pricing model, not EC2 itself. The key word is 'virtual' — EC2 uses a hypervisor for multi-tenancy on shared physical hardware.

jsonData.question: A company needs to run a high-frequency trading application that performs millions of floating-point financial calculations per second. Which EC2 instance type is most appropriate?
options: General Purpose — balanced compute, memory, and networking for diverse workloads
Memory Optimized — designed for workloads processing large datasets in memory
Storage Optimized — high sequential read/write to large local datasets with high IOPS
Compute Optimized — high-performance processors for compute-intensive applications
jsonData.explanation: Compute Optimized instances provide high-performance processors specifically for compute-intensive workloads like scientific modeling, batch processing, and high-frequency trading that require maximum CPU throughput. Memory Optimized would be chosen for workloads that load large datasets into RAM (e.g., in-memory databases). General Purpose would be balanced but not specialized enough for maximum floating-point throughput.

jsonData.question: A company runs a batch processing workload that can be paused and restarted if interrupted. The workload runs sporadically with no fixed schedule and tolerates interruptions. Which EC2 pricing model provides the maximum cost savings?
options: Spot Instances using spare EC2 capacity, offering up to 90% savings for interruption-tolerant workloads
On-Demand Instances with no upfront cost or long-term contract
Reserved Instances with a 1-year term commitment to a specific instance type
Dedicated Hosts with full physical server allocation for compliance needs
jsonData.explanation: Spot Instances provide up to 90% savings over On-Demand by using spare AWS capacity, making them the best choice for batch jobs that can tolerate a 2-minute reclaim warning. Reserved Instances offer up to 72% but require a 1 or 3-year commitment — unsuitable for sporadic workloads. On-Demand is the baseline with no savings. Dedicated Hosts are the most expensive option designed for compliance, not cost savings.

jsonData.question: What is the key difference between Reserved Instances and Savings Plans when reducing EC2 costs?
options: Reserved Instances offer higher savings (up to 90%) compared to Savings Plans (up to 72%)
Reserved Instances lock you to a specific instance type and Region, while Savings Plans offer flexibility across EC2, Lambda, and Fargate compute services
Savings Plans apply only to EC2; Reserved Instances extend savings to Lambda and Fargate as well
Savings Plans require a 3-year minimum commitment, while Reserved Instances can be purchased for 1 year
jsonData.explanation: The key distinction is flexibility: Reserved Instances commit you to a specific instance type and AWS Region (less flexible), while Savings Plans commit you to a dollar-per-hour usage amount that applies flexibly across EC2, Lambda, and Fargate. Both offer up to 72% savings — the 90% figure belongs to Spot Instances, not Reserved Instances. Option C has the services backwards.

jsonData.question: In an EC2 Auto Scaling group, what does the 'desired capacity' setting represent?
options: The target number of instances the Auto Scaling group attempts to maintain at any given time
The absolute upper limit on instances that can be launched regardless of demand
The number of instances that will always remain running, even when demand is zero
The minimum number of instances required before the load balancer accepts any traffic
jsonData.explanation: Desired capacity is the target the Auto Scaling group aims to maintain — it defaults to the minimum capacity if not explicitly set. Maximum capacity is the ceiling (option B). Minimum capacity is the floor — instances that always run (option C). The load balancer accepts traffic independently of the desired capacity setting (option D).

jsonData.question: A retail company experiences predictable traffic spikes every Friday evening due to a weekly sale event. Which Auto Scaling approach best handles this pattern, and why?
options: Dynamic scaling, which immediately reacts to real-time traffic increases as they occur
Manual scaling, because the schedule is predictable so automated scaling is unnecessary
Reserved Instances, which guarantee consistent compute capacity for regular workloads
Predictive scaling, which proactively schedules capacity before the anticipated demand arrives
jsonData.explanation: Predictive scaling analyzes historical patterns and schedules capacity proactively before demand arrives — ideal for recurring, predictable spikes like a weekly Friday sale. Dynamic scaling reacts after demand has already increased (reactive), meaning instances may not be ready in time. Reserved Instances are a pricing model, not a scaling strategy. Manual scaling defeats the purpose of automation.

jsonData.question: Which property of Elastic Load Balancing (ELB) makes it inherently highly available without requiring additional user configuration?
options: ELB is a regional construct that automatically spans Availability Zones within the Region
ELB automatically creates replacement EC2 instances if any backend instance fails
ELB stores a redundant copy of all incoming traffic in Amazon S3 for disaster recovery
ELB encrypts all traffic by default using AWS-managed TLS certificates
jsonData.explanation: ELB is a regional AWS service, meaning it is automatically distributed across all Availability Zones in the Region — inherent HA with no additional setup required. ELB does not create new EC2 instances (that is Auto Scaling's job). It does not archive traffic to S3. TLS encryption is configurable but not automatic.

jsonData.question: A company wants to send a confirmation email, update inventory in a backend service, and log a transaction — all triggered by a single purchase event. Which AWS messaging approach delivers the event to all three targets simultaneously?
options: Amazon SQS with three separate queues, one for each downstream service
Amazon SNS with a single topic and three subscribers, using fan-out delivery
Amazon SQS with a single queue that each service polls independently
AWS Lambda triggered directly by the purchase event, without any messaging layer
jsonData.explanation: Amazon SNS uses a publish/subscribe model with fan-out: one message published to a topic is delivered simultaneously to all subscribers (email service, inventory service, logging service). SQS with separate queues (option A) would require separate publish actions for each queue. A single SQS queue (option C) delivers each message to only one consumer — the first to retrieve it. Lambda without a messaging layer creates tight coupling.

jsonData.question: What is the fundamental architectural difference between Amazon SQS and Amazon SNS?
options: SQS supports multiple concurrent subscribers while SNS delivers messages to only one consumer
SNS stores messages persistently until all consumers have confirmed receipt; SQS delivers immediately without storage
SQS uses a pull model where one consumer retrieves each message; SNS uses a push model that broadcasts to all subscribers simultaneously
SNS is designed for batch processing workloads while SQS is designed for real-time push notifications
jsonData.explanation: SQS is a queue (pull model): messages are stored until one consumer retrieves and deletes them — point-to-point. SNS is pub/sub (push model): a message published to a topic is pushed to all subscribers at once (fan-out). Option A has SQS and SNS swapped. Option B describes SNS incorrectly — SNS does not persist messages after delivery. Option D reverses the actual use cases.

jsonData.question: A developer proposes using AWS Lambda to transcode large video files, where each job takes approximately 25 minutes. Why is Lambda NOT suitable for this use case?
options: Lambda does not support binary file formats and can only process text and JSON payloads
Lambda functions have a maximum execution time of 15 minutes, making long-running transcoding jobs impossible
Lambda cannot access files stored in Amazon S3, so video input files cannot be retrieved
Lambda does not scale automatically and would require manual provisioning for concurrent transcode jobs
jsonData.explanation: Lambda has a hard maximum execution duration of 15 minutes per invocation — a 25-minute transcode job would be terminated before completion. The correct alternatives are EC2 or containers (ECS/EKS/Fargate), which have no time limit. Lambda does support binary data and S3 access, and it scales automatically — options A, C, and D are all incorrect misconceptions about Lambda.

jsonData.question: What is the key distinction between Amazon ECS and Amazon EKS for container orchestration?
options: ECS supports Docker containers while EKS supports only proprietary AWS container image formats
ECS uses AWS-native orchestration and API calls; EKS runs managed Kubernetes and is compatible with open-source tooling and the Kubernetes ecosystem
EKS requires a physical connection to an on-premises data center while ECS runs entirely in the cloud
ECS is a serverless container service while EKS always requires EC2 instances for the control plane
jsonData.explanation: Both ECS and EKS orchestrate Docker containers. The key difference is the orchestration layer: ECS is AWS-native and uses AWS-specific API calls to manage containers; EKS runs fully managed Kubernetes and integrates with the open-source Kubernetes ecosystem, tooling, and community updates. Choose ECS for AWS-native simplicity; choose EKS for Kubernetes portability and open-source compatibility.

jsonData.question: A company needs to run a containerized application requiring 8 hours of continuous execution. The engineering team wants to avoid provisioning or managing any EC2 instances. Which compute service is most appropriate?
options: AWS Fargate with ECS or EKS, providing serverless container execution without any EC2 instance management
AWS Lambda, which handles all server management and is ideal for long-duration containerized tasks
EC2 with Auto Scaling, which automates instance management so the team never touches servers manually
EC2 Spot Instances, which reduce cost and remove the need for manual instance lifecycle management
jsonData.explanation: AWS Fargate is a serverless compute engine for containers — you run ECS or EKS workloads without provisioning, patching, or managing EC2 instances. Lambda has a 15-minute hard limit, ruling it out for an 8-hour job. EC2 with Auto Scaling still requires the team to configure and manage EC2 instances. Spot Instances are a pricing model — they still require managing EC2 instances.

jsonData.question: Which of the following best describes an AWS Region?
options: A geographically isolated area where AWS builds clusters of data centers
A single data center running AWS services, completely isolated from all other data centers
A global network of edge servers used to cache content closer to customers worldwide
A logical partition within an AWS data center used to isolate individual customer workloads
jsonData.explanation: An AWS Region is a geographically isolated area (e.g., us-east-1, eu-west-1) containing multiple data center clusters called Availability Zones. Option B describes a single data center — not a Region, which contains multiple AZs. Option C describes Edge Locations used by CloudFront. Option D sounds like it could describe a VPC, not a Region.

jsonData.question: A German financial services company must store customer account data within Germany due to banking regulations. Which Region selection factor takes highest priority in this scenario?
options: Pricing — the Frankfurt Region may offer lower costs than other European Regions
Proximity to customers — choosing the Region geographically closest to German bank branches
Available services — ensuring the Region supports all required AWS services for the application
Compliance and legal requirements — data must remain within Germany's legal jurisdiction
jsonData.explanation: Compliance and legal requirements always take highest priority when regulations mandate data residency. German banking regulations may require that data never leave German soil, making the eu-central-1 (Frankfurt) Region the only valid choice regardless of cost or proximity. AWS's regional data sovereignty principle ensures data never leaves a Region without explicit permission.

jsonData.question: What is the key difference between an Availability Zone (AZ) and an AWS Region?
options: A Region is a single data center; an Availability Zone is a cluster of Regions grouped together
An Availability Zone is a single data center or group of data centers within a Region; a Region is a geographically isolated area containing multiple isolated AZs
Availability Zones are accessible only via AWS Direct Connect; Regions are accessible over the public internet
A Region stores data permanently across multiple facilities; Availability Zones are temporary compute resources
jsonData.explanation: An AWS Region is a geographically isolated area (e.g., a country or part of a country) containing multiple Availability Zones. Each AZ is one or more physically separate data centers within that Region, isolated from each other by tens of miles for fault isolation. The Region/AZ hierarchy is: Region > multiple AZs > data centers. Options A, C, and D all misrepresent this hierarchy.

jsonData.question: What is the primary purpose of AWS Edge Locations in the context of Amazon CloudFront?
options: To host EC2 instances in geographic proximity to end users for lower compute latency
To cache copies of content closer to customers worldwide, reducing delivery latency regardless of origin location
To provide redundant DNS name resolution that backs up Amazon Route 53 in case of failures
To store EBS snapshots in multiple geographic locations for faster disaster recovery restoration
jsonData.explanation: Edge Locations are sites used by Amazon CloudFront (AWS's CDN) to cache copies of content — videos, images, web pages — physically closer to end users. When a user requests content, CloudFront serves it from the nearest edge location rather than the origin server, dramatically reducing latency. Edge Locations do not run EC2 instances or store EBS snapshots; Route 53 also runs at edge locations but for DNS, not as a backup.

jsonData.question: What is the key difference between AWS CloudFormation and AWS Elastic Beanstalk for deploying applications?
options: CloudFormation supports only EC2 and networking resources; Elastic Beanstalk supports all AWS service types
Elastic Beanstalk uses declarative JSON/YAML templates to define infrastructure; CloudFormation uses a visual console
CloudFormation requires manual rollback on deployment errors; Elastic Beanstalk always reverts changes automatically

CloudFormation provisions infrastructure declaratively using IaC templates with full resource control; Elastic Beanstalk handles capacity, load balancing, scaling, and health monitoring automatically from application code
jsonData.explanation: CloudFormation is an Infrastructure as Code (IaC) tool — you write JSON/YAML templates declaring the desired state, and CloudFormation provisions everything automatically with rollback on error. Elastic Beanstalk is a higher-level deployment service — you provide your application code and configuration, and Beanstalk handles the underlying infrastructure (EC2, ELB, Auto Scaling). CloudFormation gives full control; Beanstalk trades control for simplicity. Options A, B, and C all misstate what each service does.

jsonData.question: A DevOps team needs to provision identical infrastructure in 10 different AWS accounts across 3 Regions. Which provisioning method minimizes human error and best ensures consistency?
options: AWS Management Console — the visual interface makes it easy to verify each resource as it is created
AWS CLI — scripts can be run in each account to provision resources in a repeatable way
AWS CloudFormation — declarative IaC templates guarantee identical, automated provisioning across accounts and Regions
AWS Elastic Beanstalk — it automatically handles all infrastructure provisioning for the application
jsonData.explanation: CloudFormation uses declarative templates that define infrastructure as code — the same template run in any account or Region produces identical results, with automatic rollback on errors and the lowest human error risk. The Management Console requires manual clicks in each account (high error risk). CLI scripts are repeatable but still procedural and error-prone. Elastic Beanstalk manages app deployment, not arbitrary infrastructure across multiple accounts.

jsonData.question: What does Amazon VPC (Virtual Private Cloud) provide?
options: A logically isolated private network within the AWS Cloud where you control IP ranges, subnets, routing, and gateways
A virtual firewall that controls inbound and outbound traffic for individual EC2 instances
A managed DNS service that routes end users to internet applications hosted on AWS or elsewhere
A distributed in-memory caching layer that reduces database load for high-traffic applications
jsonData.explanation: Amazon VPC is your own isolated private network within AWS — you define the IP address range, create subnets, configure route tables, and attach gateways. Option B describes a Security Group (instance-level firewall). Option C describes Amazon Route 53. Option D describes Amazon ElastiCache. VPC is the foundational networking construct; all other services are placed inside it.

jsonData.question: Which of the following correctly describes the difference between a public subnet and a private subnet in an Amazon VPC?
options: Public subnets use IPv6 addressing; private subnets are limited to IPv4 addressing
Public subnets have an internet gateway attached, allowing public internet access; private subnets have no internet gateway and host internal resources like databases
Public subnets are accessible only from within the VPC; private subnets are accessible from the public internet
Public subnets store static web content; private subnets handle dynamic application business logic
jsonData.explanation: The defining difference is internet gateway access: a public subnet is associated with an internet gateway (IGW), enabling resources like web servers to receive and send internet traffic. A private subnet has no IGW attachment, so resources (like databases) are only reachable through internal VPC routing. Options A and C have the definitions inverted or fabricated. Option D confuses networking concepts with application architecture.

jsonData.question: An EC2 instance sends an HTTPS response back to a client after receiving a request. Which statement correctly describes how Network ACLs and Security Groups handle this return traffic?
options: Both Network ACLs and Security Groups automatically allow return traffic for any established connection
Both Network ACLs and Security Groups require explicit outbound rules to permit any return traffic
Network ACLs automatically allow return traffic; Security Groups always require explicit outbound rules
Security Groups automatically allow return traffic (stateful); Network ACLs must explicitly permit the return packet because they are stateless
jsonData.explanation: Security Groups are stateful — they remember that an inbound connection was established, and automatically allow the corresponding return traffic without an explicit outbound rule. Network ACLs are stateless — they evaluate every packet independently, so return traffic must match an explicit outbound allow rule. This stateful/stateless distinction is the most critical difference between these two firewall types.

jsonData.question: Which of the following is NOT a characteristic of Network ACLs compared to Security Groups?
options: Network ACLs operate at the subnet level rather than at the individual EC2 instance level
Network ACLs evaluate all inbound and outbound traffic crossing the subnet boundary
Network ACLs remember previous packet decisions to automatically allow return traffic
Network ACLs support both Allow and Deny rules, unlike Security Groups which support Allow rules only
jsonData.explanation: Option C describes stateful behavior — which belongs to Security Groups, NOT Network ACLs. ACLs are stateless: they have no memory of previous packets and must re-evaluate every packet in both directions, including return traffic. Options A and B are true ACL characteristics. Option D is also true — ACLs support explicit Deny rules while Security Groups support only Allow rules (everything else is implicitly denied).

jsonData.question: A healthcare company must connect its on-premises data center to AWS to process patient records. HIPAA compliance requires a private connection that does NOT traverse the public internet. Which connectivity option satisfies this requirement?
options: Virtual Private Gateway — creates an encrypted VPN tunnel that still routes through the public internet
Amazon CloudFront — caches sensitive content at nearby edge locations for private delivery
Internet Gateway — connects the VPC directly to the public internet for on-premises access
AWS Direct Connect — a dedicated private fiber connection between on-premises and AWS that bypasses the public internet entirely
jsonData.explanation: AWS Direct Connect provides a dedicated private physical fiber connection from your data center to AWS — traffic never touches the public internet, meeting strict compliance requirements like HIPAA. A Virtual Private Gateway creates a VPN that is encrypted but still traverses shared public internet bandwidth. CloudFront is a CDN, not a private connectivity solution. An Internet Gateway enables public internet access — the opposite of what compliance requires.

jsonData.question: Which of the following capabilities does Amazon Route 53 provide?
options: Caching frequently accessed DynamoDB queries at network edge locations to reduce database load
Encrypting DNS queries between Route 53 and CloudFront to prevent man-in-the-middle interception
Storing DNS zone records in Amazon S3 buckets for global high-durability access
Translating domain names into IP addresses and routing users to applications using multiple routing policies such as latency-based, geolocation, and weighted round robin
jsonData.explanation: Amazon Route 53 is AWS's DNS web service: it resolves domain names to IP addresses and supports sophisticated routing policies including latency-based (route to fastest Region), geolocation DNS (route based on user location), geoproximity, and weighted round robin. It can also register domain names. Options A, B, and C describe capabilities Route 53 does not have — DynamoDB caching belongs to DAX, not Route 53.

jsonData.question: A developer accidentally terminates an EC2 instance that was using an Instance Store as its primary data storage. What happens to the data stored in the Instance Store?
options: The data is automatically backed up to Amazon S3 before the instance terminates
The data persists on the physical host and can be re-attached to a new EC2 instance in the same AZ
The data is encrypted and stored in an EBS volume until an administrator manually deletes it
The data is permanently lost because Instance Store is temporary storage physically tied to the host and lost on stop or termination
jsonData.explanation: Instance Store provides temporary block-level storage physically attached to the host machine. When an EC2 instance is stopped or terminated, the instance may be assigned to a different physical host — and the original host's storage is not preserved. There is no automatic S3 backup, no persistent EBS copy, and no way to recover the data. This is why Instance Stores are only suitable for temporary data (caches, buffers, scratch data) that can be recreated.

jsonData.question: How do incremental EBS Snapshots differ from full backups?
options: Incremental snapshots are stored within a single Availability Zone; full backups are automatically replicated across multiple AZs
Incremental snapshots capture only the blocks that changed since the last snapshot; full backups copy all data in the volume every time
Incremental snapshots require the EC2 instance to be stopped; full backups can be taken while the instance is running
Incremental snapshots are automatically moved to S3 Glacier after 30 days; full backups remain in S3 Standard permanently
jsonData.explanation: EBS Snapshots are incremental: the first snapshot copies all data, but each subsequent snapshot saves only the blocks that changed since the previous one. This makes them storage-efficient compared to full backups, which copy the entire volume every time regardless of what changed. All EBS Snapshots are stored in S3 automatically. The instance does not need to be stopped for a snapshot. Option D fabricates a lifecycle behavior.

jsonData.question: A company stores audit logs in Amazon S3 that must be retained for 7 years due to legal requirements. The logs are rarely accessed but must be retrievable within 24 hours when needed. Which S3 storage class is most cost-effective?
options: S3 Standard — frequent access with 11-nines durability stored across 3 or more AZs
S3 Standard-IA — infrequent access storage with multi-AZ redundancy at a lower storage cost
S3 Glacier Deep Archive — the lowest-cost archival class with data retrieval within 12 hours
S3 One Zone-IA — lower-cost infrequent access stored in a single Availability Zone
jsonData.explanation: S3 Glacier Deep Archive is the lowest-cost S3 storage class, designed for data retained for 7–10 years that is rarely accessed. Its maximum retrieval time of up to 12 hours satisfies the 24-hour requirement. S3 Standard is designed for frequently accessed data (unnecessary cost). Standard-IA charges higher retrieval fees and is not the cheapest for very long-term archiving. One Zone-IA risks data loss if that AZ fails — unsuitable for legal compliance records.

jsonData.question: A company runs a content management system where 50 EC2 instances need to simultaneously read and write to a shared set of configuration files. Which storage service is most appropriate?
options: Amazon EBS — persistent block storage volumes attached to each individual EC2 instance
Amazon Instance Store — temporary high-performance block storage on each EC2 host machine
Amazon EFS — a regional file storage system allowing concurrent read/write access from multiple instances
Amazon S3 — object storage accessible to any client via HTTP/S API calls
jsonData.explanation: Amazon EFS is the correct choice for shared concurrent file access: it is a regional, scalable file system that multiple EC2 instances can mount simultaneously and read/write concurrently. EBS volumes can only be attached to one EC2 instance at a time (not shared across 50 instances). Instance Store is temporary. S3 is object storage that requires a full object re-upload on change — unsuitable for configuration files edited frequently by many instances.

jsonData.question: What is a key limitation of Amazon S3 when managing frequently edited large files, compared to Amazon EBS?
options: S3 does not support versioning, so previous file versions cannot be recovered after an edit
S3 stores objects in a single Availability Zone, creating a data loss risk during AZ failures
S3 requires re-uploading the entire object whenever any part changes; EBS updates only the modified blocks at the block level
S3 is accessible only to EC2 instances in the same VPC and cannot serve content to external users
jsonData.explanation: S3 uses object-level storage: the unit of write is the entire object. Even a 1-byte change in a 5 GB file requires re-uploading the full 5 GB. EBS uses block-level storage and updates only the changed blocks — efficient for databases and files with frequent small edits. Option A is false — S3 supports versioning. Option B is false — S3 Standard stores data across 3+ AZs with 11-nines durability. Option D is false — S3 is a public internet service accessible globally.

jsonData.question: An e-commerce application needs to serve 1 million product catalog lookups per second with single-digit millisecond response times. All queries are simple key-based lookups — no table joins are required. Which database service is most appropriate?
options: Amazon RDS with MySQL — structured relational data with full SQL query support
Amazon Aurora — high-availability relational database with MySQL/PostgreSQL compatibility
Amazon Redshift — optimized for analytical queries across large historical datasets
Amazon DynamoDB — serverless key-value NoSQL database with millisecond performance at any scale
jsonData.explanation: DynamoDB is purpose-built for this scenario: serverless, key-value NoSQL with single-digit millisecond response times at any scale — handling millions of requests per second without performance degradation. RDS and Aurora are relational databases optimized for complex SQL joins, not raw key-value throughput at this scale. Redshift is a data warehouse for historical analytics, not real-time operational lookups.

jsonData.question: What is the key trade-off between Amazon RDS and Amazon DynamoDB regarding schema and scalability?
options: RDS scales horizontally to unlimited capacity automatically; DynamoDB requires manual vertical scaling
DynamoDB supports complex multi-table SQL joins and aggregations; RDS is limited to simple key-value lookups

RDS uses a rigid structured schema with SQL for complex relational queries but can degrade under extreme scale; DynamoDB has a flexible schema maintaining single-digit millisecond performance at any scale
RDS is fully serverless with no capacity planning required; DynamoDB requires pre-provisioned read and write capacity units
jsonData.explanation: RDS's strength (rich SQL with complex joins) comes with a scalability trade-off — performance degrades under extreme load. DynamoDB's flexibility (no fixed schema) and horizontal auto-scaling deliver consistent millisecond response at massive scale, but without relational join capabilities. Options A and B reverse the actual characteristics of each service. Option D is backwards — DynamoDB is the serverless option; RDS manages servers under the hood.

jsonData.question: Which of the following correctly describes Amazon Aurora's high-availability architecture?
options: Aurora stores 2 copies of data in each of 2 Availability Zones for a total of 4 redundant copies
Aurora replicates 6 copies of data across 3 Availability Zones and continuously backs up to Amazon S3
Aurora stores a primary copy in one data center with automatic failover to a secondary on-premises location
Aurora uses 3 copies of data distributed across 3 separate AWS Regions for cross-Region disaster recovery
jsonData.explanation: Aurora maintains 6 copies of data across 3 Availability Zones (2 copies per AZ), continuously backing up to Amazon S3 with point-in-time recovery. It also supports up to 15 read replicas. This architecture allows Aurora to sustain the loss of up to 2 copies without affecting write availability. Options A, C, and D all misstate the actual number of copies, the location tier (AZ vs. Region vs. on-premises), or the configuration.

jsonData.question: A business intelligence team needs to run complex analytical queries on 5 years of historical sales data aggregated from multiple source systems. Queries scan billions of rows and currently take hours on the operational relational database. Which AWS service should replace the current analytics system?
options: Amazon DynamoDB — for single-digit millisecond performance on key-based lookups at any scale
Amazon RDS with read replicas — to offload analytical queries from the primary database
Amazon Redshift — a data warehousing service delivering up to 10× better performance than traditional databases for BI workloads at petabyte scale
Amazon ElastiCache — to cache the results of common analytical queries and reduce database load
jsonData.explanation: Amazon Redshift is AWS's data warehouse, purpose-built for complex analytical queries on massive historical datasets from multiple sources — exactly this scenario. It delivers up to 10× better performance than traditional relational databases for BI workloads and scales to petabytes. DynamoDB is for simple operational key-value lookups, not complex historical analytics. RDS read replicas reduce operational load but don't overcome the performance ceiling for billion-row analytical scans. ElastiCache caches results but does not replace the underlying analytical engine.

jsonData.question: A company is migrating its Oracle database to Amazon Aurora PostgreSQL. Which migration steps are required, and why?
options: Export the Oracle database to S3, then import directly into Aurora without any schema conversion tools

First use the AWS Schema Conversion Tool to convert the schema and application code, then use AWS DMS to migrate the data — because Oracle and Aurora PostgreSQL use incompatible engines requiring a heterogeneous migration
Use AWS DMS directly without schema conversion — Oracle and PostgreSQL schemas are automatically compatible
Deploy AWS Elastic Beanstalk as a translation middleware layer between Oracle and Aurora schemas
jsonData.explanation: Oracle to Aurora PostgreSQL is a heterogeneous migration (different database engines), which requires two steps: (1) AWS Schema Conversion Tool converts the Oracle schema, stored procedures, and code to PostgreSQL-compatible equivalents; (2) AWS DMS migrates the actual data while keeping the source Oracle database operational. A homogeneous migration (e.g., MySQL to MySQL) would require only DMS. Direct S3 export and Elastic Beanstalk are not database migration tools.

jsonData.question: A social media company needs to store user relationships (followers, following, mutual connections) and run traversal queries like 'find all friends-of-friends within 3 relationship hops.' Which AWS database service is designed for this use case?
options: Amazon QLDB — an immutable ledger database for maintaining a complete, auditable history of data changes
Amazon DynamoDB — a serverless key-value NoSQL database for high-speed, high-scale lookups
Amazon Neptune — a graph database service optimized for highly connected datasets and relationship traversal queries
Amazon DocumentDB — a MongoDB-compatible document database for hierarchical JSON data structures
jsonData.explanation: Amazon Neptune is a fully managed graph database designed for highly connected data where relationships between entities are as important as the data itself. Social networks, recommendation engines, fraud detection, and knowledge graphs are Neptune's primary use cases. QLDB is for audit trails. DynamoDB handles key-value lookups efficiently but is not optimized for multi-hop relationship traversal. DocumentDB stores documents, not graph relationships.

jsonData.question: A media company uploads 10,000 video files daily. Videos are accessed frequently for the first 30 days after upload, rarely for the next 90 days, then must be preserved for 7 years for licensing compliance. Which approach minimizes storage cost while meeting all requirements?
options: Store all videos in S3 Standard permanently and enable versioning to satisfy the compliance retention requirement
Store all videos directly in S3 Glacier from upload to minimize cost from day one
Use S3 Lifecycle Policies to automatically transition objects: S3 Standard (days 0–30) → S3 Standard-IA (days 31–120) → S3 Glacier Deep Archive (day 120 onward)
Store active videos on EC2 instance store volumes for performance, then manually archive to S3 Glacier after 30 days
jsonData.explanation: S3 Lifecycle Policies automate cost optimization by transitioning objects between storage classes based on age — no code changes required. S3 Standard during peak access (days 0–30), Standard-IA for the low-access period (days 31–120), then Glacier Deep Archive for the 7-year compliance archive (cheapest class for rarely accessed long-term data). Storing everything in Standard forever is expensive. Moving to Glacier immediately ignores the 30-day frequent-access window. Instance store is ephemeral — data would be lost on instance stop.

jsonData.question: A company's web tier sends purchase requests directly to the fulfillment service over a network call. When the fulfillment service experiences an outage, the web tier crashes with connection timeout errors. Which redesign best resolves this tight coupling?
options: Deploy the fulfillment service on Reserved Instances to guarantee 99.99% uptime and eliminate outage risk
Add a Virtual Private Gateway between the web tier and fulfillment service for encrypted, reliable communication

Place an Amazon SQS queue between the web tier and fulfillment service so each component operates independently — the web tier writes orders to the queue and the fulfillment service reads them when available
Use AWS CloudFormation to automatically redeploy the fulfillment service within seconds of any detected failure
jsonData.explanation: The root cause is tight coupling — direct synchronous calls mean the web tier's availability depends on the fulfillment service. Adding an SQS queue creates a loosely coupled architecture: the web tier writes to the queue and continues operating even if the fulfillment service is down; the fulfillment service processes messages when it recovers, with no messages lost. Reserved Instances improve uptime but don't eliminate outages. A Virtual Private Gateway is a hybrid networking tool, not a decoupling mechanism. CloudFormation manages infrastructure, not failure isolation between application tiers.

jsonData.question: Which of the following best defines cloud computing?
options: The deployment of physical servers in third-party data centers under a leasing agreement
The use of virtualization technology within a company's own on-premises data center
The on-demand delivery of IT resources over the internet with pay-as-you-go pricing
A model in which all computing resources are shared among users within a single organization
jsonData.explanation: Cloud computing is defined as 'the on-demand delivery of IT resources and applications through the internet with pay-as-you-go pricing.' Option A describes colocation hosting — renting physical space, not cloud services. Option B describes private cloud (on-premises) virtualization, which is a separate deployment model.

jsonData.question: In the client-server model, which of the following best describes a 'client'?
options: A web browser or desktop application that a person interacts with to make requests to a server
A physical or virtual server that evaluates and fulfils incoming requests from users
A network router that directs traffic between clients and servers within a data center
An API gateway that translates user requests into machine-readable server instructions
jsonData.explanation: A client is a web browser or desktop application that a person interacts with to make requests to computer servers. Amazon EC2 is an example of a server (the other side of the model), not a client. Network routers and API gateways are infrastructure components that assist communication but are not the client in this model.

jsonData.question: How does the 'variable expense' model of cloud computing differ from the 'upfront expense' model of traditional on-premises infrastructure?
options: Variable expense requires a long-term contract, while upfront expense is billed on a monthly basis
Variable expense is more expensive in total but spreads payments evenly over time to aid budgeting
Upfront expense is more predictable and is therefore preferred for large-scale enterprise deployments
Variable expense means paying only for resources actually consumed, while upfront expense requires a large capital investment in hardware before knowing actual usage
jsonData.explanation: Variable expense in cloud computing means you pay only for the compute resources you consume — no need to invest heavily in data centers and servers before knowing how you will use them. Upfront expense requires purchasing hardware before deployment, locking capital into assets regardless of actual utilization. Option C is incorrect — the module presents variable expense as an advantage, not upfront expense.

jsonData.question: What is the alternative name for 'on-premises deployment' in cloud computing terminology?
options: Hybrid cloud deployment
Private cloud deployment
Community cloud deployment
Multi-cloud deployment
jsonData.explanation: On-premises deployment is also known as 'private cloud' deployment. In this model, resources are deployed on premises using virtualization and resource management tools. This terminology equivalence is important — exam questions may use either term to describe the same deployment model.

jsonData.question: In the context of AWS and the client-server model, Amazon EC2 is best described as fulfilling which role?
options: A client application that users interact with to request AWS cloud resources
A network switch that routes traffic between AWS data centers and end users
A type of virtual server that evaluates and fulfils requests made by client applications
A DNS resolver that translates domain names into IP addresses for connecting clients
jsonData.explanation: Amazon EC2 (Elastic Compute Cloud) is described as 'a type of virtual server' in the client-server model. It is the server side: it evaluates requests and returns results to clients (web browsers, desktop applications). The module explicitly names EC2 as the server example. Confusing EC2 as a client is a common misconception.

jsonData.question: Why can AWS offer lower pay-as-you-go prices than what individual companies typically achieve by running their own infrastructure?
options: The aggregated cloud usage from hundreds of thousands of customers allows AWS to achieve economies of scale, reducing the variable cost per unit for all customers
AWS uses lower-quality commodity hardware that significantly reduces procurement and depreciation costs
AWS receives government subsidies and tax incentives that allow it to offer services below true market cost
Individual companies overspend on premium enterprise hardware, while AWS uses standard off-the-shelf components
jsonData.explanation: Economies of scale occur because AWS aggregates usage from hundreds of thousands of customers. This massive combined scale allows AWS to negotiate better hardware prices and operate more efficiently than any individual company — savings that translate into lower pay-as-you-go prices. Options B and C describe false mechanisms. Option D contains a grain of truth but is not the principle the module describes.

jsonData.question: A company based in Germany needs to serve customers in Tokyo with minimal latency. Which cloud computing benefit makes this readily achievable without building their own Japanese data center?
options: Trading upfront expense for variable expense, eliminating the need to purchase Japanese server hardware
Benefiting from economies of scale by consolidating all servers in one central European location
Stopping to guess capacity by using Auto Scaling to handle Japanese traffic volumes automatically
Going global in minutes by deploying applications to AWS Regions located close to customers in Japan
jsonData.explanation: 'Go global in minutes' refers to AWS's global network of Regions, which allows deploying applications close to customers worldwide and providing low latency — even when the company and its customers are on opposite sides of the world. Options A and B do not address geographic reach or latency. Option C addresses capacity scaling, not geographic proximity.

jsonData.question: A hospital uses a patient records system that their vendor confirmed cannot be safely migrated to the cloud due to deep integration dependencies. The hospital also wants to adopt cloud services for medical image analysis. Which deployment model is most appropriate?
options: Cloud-based deployment, gradually migrating the patient records system to the cloud over several years
Hybrid deployment, keeping the patient records system on-premises while connecting cloud-based image analysis services
On-premises deployment, running both the records system and image analysis within the hospital's own data center
Multi-region deployment, separating the two workloads across different AWS Regions for isolation
jsonData.explanation: Hybrid deployment is designed exactly for this scenario — when legacy applications are better maintained on-premises (the records system that cannot be migrated), while the organization still benefits from cloud services (medical image analysis). Option A contradicts the vendor's warning. Option C gives up all cloud benefits. 'Multi-region deployment' is a strategy within cloud-based deployment, not a separate model for mixing on-premises with cloud.

jsonData.question: A team of developers is building a brand-new social media platform from scratch with no existing servers or infrastructure. They need maximum flexibility and rapid scaling. Which deployment model best fits their situation?
options: On-premises deployment to ensure they own and control all their infrastructure from day one
Hybrid deployment to preserve the option to add on-premises resources in the future
Cloud-based deployment, designing and building all application components entirely in the cloud
Private cloud deployment using their own virtualized servers in a co-located data center
jsonData.explanation: Cloud-based deployment is ideal for new applications built from scratch — it provides maximum flexibility, rapid scaling, and eliminates upfront hardware investment. The team has no legacy constraints that would require on-premises resources, making hybrid unnecessary. On-premises and private cloud would require capital investment and significantly more management, defeating their goals.

jsonData.question: A UK bank is legally required to keep all customer financial records within its own UK-based facilities and cannot store them on any external servers. The bank also wants to run cloud-based marketing analytics. Which deployment model does this scenario represent?
options: Hybrid deployment, with financial records on-premises in the UK and marketing analytics running in the cloud
On-premises deployment with a private UK data center handling all workloads including analytics
Cloud-based deployment using the AWS Europe (London) Region, which is physically located in the UK
Multi-cloud deployment, distributing workloads across cloud providers that each have UK data centers
jsonData.explanation: This is a hybrid deployment — the regulated financial records stay on-premises (satisfying the 'cannot store on external servers' requirement), while the non-regulated marketing analytics runs in the cloud. Option C is tempting but incorrect: the scenario specifies records must be on the bank's own facilities, not just in the UK — an AWS Region is still an external server. Hybrid explicitly connects on-premises and cloud.

jsonData.question: A company's IT operations team currently spends 60% of their time managing server hardware, replacing failed drives, and maintaining cooling systems. Which cloud computing benefit most directly reduces this operational burden?
options: Benefiting from massive economies of scale to lower the per-unit cost of hardware
Going global in minutes to replicate data centers across multiple regions automatically
Increasing speed and agility to provision replacement servers more quickly after failures
Stopping spending money to run and maintain data centers, freeing the team to focus on applications and customers
jsonData.explanation: 'Stop spending money to run and maintain data centers' directly targets the operational burden of managing physical infrastructure — hardware, cooling, and maintenance. With cloud computing, AWS handles this responsibility. Option A addresses cost structure, not operational burden. Option C (speed/agility) relates to development velocity, not reducing the maintenance workload itself.

jsonData.question: Which of the following is NOT listed as one of the six benefits of cloud computing?
options: Trading upfront expense for variable expense
Eliminating the need for application software development by providing pre-built cloud applications
Benefiting from massive economies of scale
Stopping spending money to run and maintain data centers
jsonData.explanation: The six cloud computing benefits are: (1) trade upfront for variable expense, (2) stop spending on data centers, (3) stop guessing capacity, (4) benefit from economies of scale, (5) increase speed and agility, and (6) go global in minutes. Cloud computing provides infrastructure — it does not replace the need to develop your own applications. Pre-built apps are a product category, not a fundamental cloud computing benefit.

jsonData.question: Before adopting cloud, a company planning its on-premises data center faces a fundamental capacity dilemma. Which statement best characterizes this dilemma?
options: Servers must be replaced every three years, creating unpredictable capital expenditure cycles that cloud avoids
On-premises hardware cannot be virtualized, limiting each physical server to running a single application
Buying for average load leaves insufficient capacity at peak times, while buying for peak load results in costly idle resources during normal operations
Data center cooling costs grow faster than compute capacity, making large on-premises deployments economically unviable
jsonData.explanation: The module explicitly describes this dilemma: 'If you buy for the average amount, you won't be wasting money on average — but when peak loads come in, you won't have the hardware to service the customers. If you buy for the top max load, you might have happy customers, but for most of the year you'll have idle resources.' This is exactly what 'stop guessing capacity' solves. Options A and D describe real costs but not the core trade-off described.

jsonData.question: What is the primary difference between 'cloud-based' and 'on-premises' deployment models regarding infrastructure responsibility?
options: In on-premises deployment, the organization manages its own infrastructure using virtualization tools; in cloud-based deployment, the cloud provider manages the underlying physical infrastructure
In cloud-based deployment, the organization must employ specialized server administrators; in on-premises deployment, AWS manages the hardware on their behalf
On-premises deployment always uses bare-metal physical servers without virtualization; cloud-based deployment exclusively uses virtual machines
Cloud-based deployment requires a minimum 3-year financial commitment to the cloud provider; on-premises has no such contractual constraint
jsonData.explanation: The core distinction is management responsibility. On-premises deployment means the organization manages its own infrastructure — hardware, virtualization, patching, and staffing — in its own facilities. Cloud-based deployment means the provider (AWS) manages the underlying physical layer. Option C is incorrect — on-premises deployments explicitly do use virtualization, as stated in the module ('using virtualization and resource management tools').

jsonData.question: A CFO insists: 'Since cloud is cheaper overall, we should migrate our entire operation immediately — including our 15-year-old ERP system.' A solutions architect disagrees. Which response best aligns with cloud deployment model principles?
options: The CFO is correct; all legacy systems benefit from cloud migration and should be moved immediately to reduce costs
The architect should recommend delaying all cloud adoption until every legacy application has been fully rewritten for cloud compatibility
The architect should recommend full on-premises deployment for all workloads until legacy systems are completely resolved
The architect should recommend hybrid deployment: keep the legacy ERP on-premises where it is better maintained, and migrate other workloads to the cloud to gain immediate cloud benefits
jsonData.explanation: Hybrid deployment is designed for organizations that have 'legacy applications that are better maintained on premises.' The module explicitly describes hybrid as the approach when some systems 'will not be migrated to the cloud' while others benefit from cloud services. Option B is overly conservative — the organization misses cloud benefits for everything. Option A ignores the real-world complexity of migrating legacy ERP systems.

jsonData.question: Company X runs its own data centers and Company Y uses AWS. Both serve the same number of customers with equivalent workloads. Which cost statement is most accurate according to cloud computing principles?
options: Company X has lower costs because it owns its infrastructure outright and pays no monthly cloud fees
Company Y likely achieves lower variable costs because AWS leverages economies of scale from aggregated usage across thousands of customers — a scale Company X alone cannot match
Both companies have equivalent per-unit costs because cloud providers charge market-rate prices for compute
Company X has more predictable costs because it purchased hardware upfront and knows its exact capital expenditure
jsonData.explanation: Economies of scale allow AWS to offer lower variable costs than individual companies can achieve alone, because AWS aggregates usage from hundreds of thousands of customers, enabling greater procurement efficiencies. Company X's single-company scale cannot match this. Option A confuses 'no monthly fees' with 'lower total cost' — ignoring the ongoing costs of hardware maintenance, power, cooling, and staffing. Option D confuses predictability of sunk costs with lower total costs.

jsonData.question: Which of the following scenarios most accurately represents the intended use case for a hybrid deployment model?
options: A company runs all its applications in two different AWS Regions for disaster recovery and redundancy
A company uses AWS for development environments and Microsoft Azure for production workloads
A company connects its cloud-based customer analytics platform to its on-premises mainframe that processes regulated financial transactions which cannot be migrated
A company uses AWS exclusively but places web servers in public subnets and databases in private subnets within the same VPC
jsonData.explanation: Hybrid deployment connects cloud-based resources to on-premises infrastructure — exactly what Option C describes: cloud analytics connected to a regulatory-constrained on-premises mainframe. Option A is a multi-region cloud-based strategy (still fully in the cloud). Option B is multi-cloud (two public cloud providers), not hybrid as the module defines it. Option D is VPC networking design within a single cloud deployment.

jsonData.question: A retail company generates 80% of its annual online sales in the four weeks before a major holiday. For the remaining 48 weeks, traffic is minimal. Which cloud computing benefit provides the MOST direct business value for this demand pattern?
options: Stopping to guess capacity: scaling resources up for the holiday peak and scaling back during off-peak periods, paying only for actual usage
Going global in minutes: deploying to additional Regions to serve international holiday shoppers more efficiently
Benefiting from economies of scale: reducing the per-transaction cost during the high-volume holiday period
Increasing speed and agility: deploying new promotional features and holiday landing pages faster
jsonData.explanation: 'Stop guessing capacity' is the most directly applicable benefit. The seasonal pattern (80% of revenue in 4 weeks) is precisely the scenario the module describes: on-premises, the company must either overbuy for peak (servers idle 48 weeks/year) or underbuy for peak (unable to handle holiday load). Cloud lets them scale up for the holiday and scale back immediately after, paying only for peak resources when actually needed. While other benefits apply, none address the core variable-demand problem as directly.

jsonData.question: A student argues: 'On-premises deployment is always inferior to cloud-based deployment because it costs more and is less flexible.' What is the flaw in this reasoning?
options: There is no flaw — on-premises deployment is universally inferior to cloud-based deployment across all use cases
On-premises deployment is actually cheaper than cloud for all workloads because companies avoid recurring monthly fees
On-premises deployment is more flexible because companies own their hardware and can modify it however they choose
On-premises deployment can be the required or best choice when regulations mandate data stays within the organization's own facilities, making it a legitimate option in specific contexts
jsonData.explanation: The module explicitly states that on-premises is appropriate when 'government regulations require your business to keep certain records on premises' and when legacy applications are 'better maintained on premises.' Calling it 'always inferior' ignores these valid use cases. Cloud-based deployment is not universally optimal — it depends on regulatory environment, application characteristics, and organizational requirements. Option B overstates the cost advantage by ignoring the total cost of on-premises ownership (staffing, power, maintenance).

jsonData.question: What does 'pay-as-you-go' pricing mean in the context of cloud computing?
options: You pay a fixed monthly subscription fee regardless of how much of the service you actually consume
You pay only for the specific computing resources you consume, with no upfront costs or minimum usage contracts required
You pay in advance for a committed block of compute capacity and draw it down over the contract period
You pay a base platform fee plus a per-unit charge that increases progressively as your usage grows
jsonData.explanation: Pay-as-you-go means you pay only for the resources you actually use — no upfront investment and no payment for idle capacity. The module states: 'you don't pay for the resource upfront; instead, you just provision and pay at the end of the month.' Option A is a subscription model. Option C is a prepaid capacity model (closer to Reserved Instances). Option D describes tiered pricing — all are different from the pay-as-you-go principle.

jsonData.question: In the AWS client-server model, which interaction correctly illustrates Amazon EC2 acting as the server?
options: EC2 connects to a user's browser to automatically push software updates and security patches
EC2 makes API calls to other AWS services to retrieve configuration information needed by an application
A user's web browser requests a news article, and EC2 evaluates the request and returns the article content
EC2 forwards incoming customer requests to Amazon S3 for object storage retrieval on their behalf
jsonData.explanation: The module's exact client-server example is: 'a client makes a request for a news article, the score in an online game, or a funny video. The server evaluates the details of this request and fulfils it by returning the information to the client.' EC2 is the server in this model — it evaluates and fulfils. Option B has EC2 acting as a client (making requests to other services), not as a server responding to user requests.

jsonData.question: Both 'trading upfront expense for variable expense' and 'benefiting from economies of scale' are cost-related cloud benefits. What is the key distinction between them?
options: Variable expense describes the customer's payment model (paying only for what is consumed), while economies of scale explain why AWS can offer lower prices in that model (aggregated usage reduces AWS's own per-unit costs)
Variable expense applies only to compute services like EC2, while economies of scale apply uniformly across all AWS service categories
Economies of scale only benefit large enterprise customers, while variable expense benefits organizations of all sizes equally
Variable expense is a benefit realized over many years of cloud usage, while economies of scale provide an immediate one-time discount upon signing up
jsonData.explanation: These two benefits operate at different levels. Variable expense describes the customer's cost structure — how they pay (only for what they use, not upfront). Economies of scale explain the provider-side mechanism — why AWS can afford to offer those lower variable rates (aggregated usage from hundreds of thousands of customers reduces AWS's per-unit costs). They are complementary: variable expense is the 'what' and economies of scale partially explain the 'why' of lower cloud pricing.

jsonData.question: An enterprise has 200 applications: 50 are modern microservices ready for cloud migration, and 150 are legacy systems with complex on-premises dependencies. A manager says: 'Let's keep everything on-premises until we can migrate everything.' What does cloud deployment strategy suggest instead?
options: The manager is correct — a mixed cloud/on-premises approach creates unnecessary operational complexity and security risk
Migrate all 200 applications to the cloud simultaneously to maximize cost savings and eliminate technical debt quickly
Keep all 200 applications on-premises and deploy a managed private cloud solution within the existing data center
Use hybrid deployment: migrate the 50 ready applications to the cloud immediately for cloud benefits, while keeping the 150 legacy systems on-premises until properly prepared
jsonData.explanation: The module states hybrid deployment is for organizations with 'legacy applications that are more suitable on premises' who still want to 'benefit from data and analytics services that run in the cloud.' Waiting for all 150 legacy systems (Option A/manager) means forgoing cloud benefits for the 50 ready applications — sometimes for years. Forcing simultaneous migration of 150 complex legacy systems (Option B) creates unacceptable risk. Hybrid is the principled approach: move what is ready, maintain what is not.

jsonData.question: A developer says: 'Cloud computing helps me get new features to market faster because I can experiment and iterate without long hardware procurement cycles.' Which cloud computing benefit does this statement primarily describe?
options: Going global in minutes, since faster deployment means reaching customers worldwide sooner after release
Increasing speed and agility, since cloud computing's flexibility makes it easier to develop, deploy, and experiment with new applications
Stopping to guess capacity, since eliminating hardware planning allows the development team to start coding immediately
Benefiting from economies of scale, since lower infrastructure costs free up budget that can be redirected to development teams
jsonData.explanation: 'Increase speed and agility' directly matches the developer's statement. The module states: 'The flexibility of cloud computing makes it easier for you to develop and deploy applications. This flexibility provides you with more time to experiment and innovate. When computing in data centres, it may take weeks to obtain new resources. By comparison, cloud computing enables you to access new resources within minutes.' Option A (go global) is about geographic reach, not development velocity.

jsonData.question: An analyst states: 'By moving to AWS, we have reduced our per-unit infrastructure cost because we are now part of a larger pool of customers whose combined usage makes the provider more operationally efficient.' Which cloud computing benefit is the analyst describing?
options: Trading upfront expense for variable expense, since paying per-unit eliminates wasted capital on unused hardware
Stopping spending money to run and maintain data centers, since AWS absorbs all infrastructure operational costs
Benefiting from massive economies of scale, since aggregated customer usage allows AWS to reduce per-unit costs and pass savings to customers
Increasing speed and agility in resource provisioning, since the pooled infrastructure is always immediately available
jsonData.explanation: The analyst is describing economies of scale — the mechanism by which AWS's aggregated usage from hundreds of thousands of customers drives down per-unit costs for everyone. The phrase 'larger pool of customers whose combined usage makes the provider more efficient' is the definition of economies of scale. This is distinct from variable expense (which is about the payment model, not the provider's efficiency) and from stopping data center spending (which is about operational burden, not unit economics).

jsonData.question: Which of the following best describes block-level storage as used by Amazon EC2 instance stores?
options: A storage type that treats files as complete discrete objects with metadata and unique keys
A distributed file system accessible concurrently by multiple clients
A storage type that saves files as small component blocks, updating only the changed blocks when a file is modified
A hierarchical archival system designed for long-term data retention
jsonData.explanation: Block-level storage breaks files into small component parts (blocks). When a file is updated, only the changed blocks are overwritten — not the entire file. This makes it efficient for databases and applications with frequent edits. Option A describes object storage (S3), not block storage.

jsonData.question: Why is data stored in an EC2 instance store lost when the instance is stopped and then restarted?
options: AWS automatically purges instance store data after 24 hours for security compliance
When an EC2 instance restarts from a stopped state, it may launch on a different physical host where that instance store volume does not exist
Instance stores use encryption keys that are deleted whenever the instance stops
Instance stores require the EC2 instance to stay in the same Region, and a Region failover resets the data
jsonData.explanation: EC2 instances are virtual machines. When stopped and restarted, the instance may start on a completely different physical host. Because instance store volumes are physically attached to the original host computer, the new host has no access to that volume — so all data is lost. This is the core reason instance stores are ephemeral.

jsonData.question: A developer needs temporary scratch space for a parallel data-processing job on an EC2 instance. The intermediate data can be easily recreated if lost. Which storage option is most appropriate and why?
options: Amazon EBS with automated snapshots, to ensure recovery if data is lost mid-job
Amazon S3 Standard, because it provides high durability and does not require an EC2 attachment
Amazon EFS, because it grows automatically and can be accessed by all cluster nodes simultaneously
EC2 instance store, because it is physically attached to the host for maximum performance and the ephemeral nature is acceptable when data can be recreated
jsonData.explanation: Instance stores are ideal precisely for temporary, easily-recreated data such as scratch files. Since the developer confirms data can be recreated, the ephemeral risk is irrelevant. Instance stores are also the highest-performance option since they are physically attached. Using EBS or S3 would add cost and complexity without benefit here.

jsonData.question: What does the acronym EBS stand for in AWS?
options: Elastic Block Store
Elastic Backup Service
Extended Block Storage
Elastic Bucket System
jsonData.explanation: EBS stands for Elastic Block Store. It is a block-level persistent storage service for EC2 instances. Unlike instance stores, EBS data persists even when an EC2 instance is stopped or terminated.

jsonData.question: How does an Amazon EBS incremental snapshot differ from a traditional full backup?
options: EBS snapshots compress all data before saving, while full backups store raw data
EBS snapshots save only the blocks that have changed since the most recent snapshot, while full backups copy all data in the volume every time
EBS snapshots are stored in S3 Glacier automatically, while full backups go to S3 Standard
EBS snapshots can only be created once per volume, while full backups can be repeated on any schedule
jsonData.explanation: An EBS snapshot is incremental: the first snapshot copies all data, but subsequent snapshots save only the blocks that changed since the last snapshot. A full backup, by contrast, copies every block each time — including data that has not changed. Incremental snapshots are faster and cheaper for ongoing backups.

jsonData.question: A video editor is making frequent small scene-level corrections to an 80 GB film file. Which AWS storage service is best suited for this workflow, and why?
options: Amazon S3 Standard, because it is serverless, regionally distributed, and has 11 nines of durability
Amazon S3 Standard-IA, because the file is edited infrequently and benefits from lower storage cost
Amazon EBS, because it uses block storage and only rewrites the changed blocks when edits are saved — avoiding re-uploading the entire 80 GB file
Amazon EFS, because it can be accessed from multiple editing workstations concurrently
jsonData.explanation: EBS uses block-level storage: when you save an edit, only the modified blocks are updated, not the whole file. If you stored this file in S3 (object storage), every save would require re-uploading the entire 80 GB because object storage has no concept of delta updates — the whole object must be replaced. For frequent partial edits, EBS clearly wins.

jsonData.question: A company wants to host millions of product images for an e-commerce site, accessible via URL with permission controls. Which AWS storage service is most appropriate?
options: Amazon S3, because every object has a URL with configurable access controls, it is regionally distributed with 11 nines durability, and no EC2 instances are needed
Amazon EBS, because block-level storage provides faster read performance for high-traffic image requests
Amazon EFS, because it allows multiple web servers to access image files from the same shared file system
EC2 instance store, because physically attached storage has the lowest latency for serving images
jsonData.explanation: S3 is the clear winner for this use case: every S3 object has a URL, access can be controlled per object, and S3 is regionally distributed with 11 nines of durability — eliminating the need for a separate backup strategy. It is also serverless, so no EC2 instances are required. EBS requires EC2 and is per-instance, making it impractical for a globally accessed image library.

jsonData.question: What is the maximum file size for a single object stored in Amazon S3?
options: 1 TB
50 GB
100 GB
5 TB
jsonData.explanation: The maximum file size for a single object in Amazon S3 is 5 TB. S3 provides unlimited total storage space, but individual objects are capped at 5 TB. This supports extremely large files such as high-resolution video archives or database backups.

jsonData.question: In Amazon S3 object storage, what are the three components that make up each stored object?
options: File path, permissions, and timestamps
Data, metadata, and a key (unique identifier)
Bucket name, Region, and encryption type
Object ID, MIME type, and block allocation table
jsonData.explanation: Every S3 object consists of: (1) the data itself (the file content — image, video, document, etc.), (2) metadata describing the object (size, content type, usage info), and (3) a key, which is the unique identifier for the object within its bucket. Understanding these three components is fundamental to how S3 differs from file and block storage.

jsonData.question: Compared to S3 Standard, S3 Standard-Infrequent Access (S3 Standard-IA) has which pricing characteristic?
options: Higher storage price and lower retrieval price
Same storage price but adds a retrieval fee per GB downloaded
Lower storage price and higher retrieval price
Lower storage price with no retrieval charges whatsoever
jsonData.explanation: S3 Standard-IA trades lower storage cost for higher retrieval cost. This makes it ideal for data you keep long-term but rarely access (like backups or disaster recovery files) — you save money storing it, but pay more when you need to retrieve it. Option A describes the opposite pricing, which is incorrect.

jsonData.question: A company needs to store backup files that can be recreated if an Availability Zone fails. They want the lowest possible storage price. Which S3 storage class should they use?
options: S3 One Zone-IA, because it stores data in a single AZ at a lower price than S3 Standard-IA and is acceptable when data can be recreated
S3 Standard, because it provides maximum reliability across three AZs
S3 Glacier, because archival classes always have the lowest storage price
S3 Intelligent-Tiering, because it automatically minimizes costs for all access patterns
jsonData.explanation: S3 One Zone-IA is the right choice when two conditions are met: you want lower cost than Standard-IA, and you can afford to lose the data if an AZ fails (because you can recreate it). Unlike S3 Standard and Standard-IA which store data across at least three AZs, One Zone-IA uses only one AZ — the trade-off that enables its lower price.

jsonData.question: In S3 Intelligent-Tiering, what happens automatically when an object has NOT been accessed for 30 consecutive days?
options: The object is permanently deleted to reduce storage costs
The object is moved to S3 Glacier for long-term archival
An additional copy of the object is created in a second AZ for durability
The object is moved to the infrequent access tier (equivalent to S3 Standard-IA)
jsonData.explanation: S3 Intelligent-Tiering monitors each object's access patterns. If an object goes unaccessed for 30 consecutive days, it automatically moves to the infrequent access tier to reduce storage cost. If accessed again, it moves back to the frequent access tier (S3 Standard). This is a key feature for data with unpredictable or changing access patterns.

jsonData.question: A healthcare company must retain patient records for 7 years per regulation, with a hard requirement that records cannot be altered or deleted after being written. Which S3 feature best addresses this?
options: S3 Versioning with MFA delete, to require multi-factor authentication before any deletion
S3 Glacier vault lock with a write once/read many (WORM) policy locked from future edits
S3 Intelligent-Tiering with a lifecycle rule set to transition objects after 7 years
S3 Standard with restrictive IAM policies that deny all delete operations
jsonData.explanation: S3 Glacier vault lock policies allow you to enforce WORM (write once, read many) controls. Once a vault lock policy is locked, it cannot be changed — not even by the account owner. This immutability guarantee satisfies compliance requirements for records retention. IAM policies (option D) can be changed by an administrator, so they don't provide the same immutability guarantee.

jsonData.question: What is the key operational difference between S3 Glacier and S3 Glacier Deep Archive?
options: S3 Glacier retrieves data within seconds, while S3 Glacier Deep Archive takes minutes to hours
S3 Glacier stores data in a single AZ, while S3 Glacier Deep Archive uses multiple AZs
S3 Glacier retrieves objects within a few minutes to a few hours, while S3 Glacier Deep Archive retrieves objects within 12 hours
S3 Glacier Deep Archive offers faster retrieval at a higher price, while S3 Glacier is slower but cheaper
jsonData.explanation: Both are archival storage classes, but they differ in retrieval speed: S3 Glacier takes minutes to a few hours, while S3 Glacier Deep Archive takes up to 12 hours. Deep Archive is the lower-cost option and is suited for data you almost never need to retrieve quickly. Option D has the price/speed relationship reversed — Deep Archive is cheaper AND slower, not faster.
